title: SEGMENT 7 — Hanna's mouse synthetic embryos: heart and brain in a dish

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Hanna's mouse synthetic embryos: heart and brain in a dish
In August 2022, Jacob Hanna's group at the Weizmann Institute published a paper in Cell titled "Post-gastrulation synthetic embryos generated ex utero from mouse naive ESCs.
" A follow-up paper in Nature in 2023 extended the work.
Taken together, these papers are, to my mind, the single most striking demonstrations the synthetic embryo field has produced.
They are the demonstrations that make people gasp when they see the videos.
The setup was this.
Hanna's group took mouse pluripotent stem cells — naive ES cells — and pushed them through a sequence of conditions designed to generate the three lineages of the early embryo.

They combined the cells in a specialized mechanical incubator — a rotating bottle system that keeps the embryo-like structures gently agitated, with carefully controlled gas concentrations and continuous medium changes.

This system had been developed in the same lab a couple of years earlier, and it had been shown to support development of real mouse embryos ex utero — outside the uterus — for substantially longer than any previous culture system.
Hanna's team applied this same system to stem-cell-derived aggregates.
And what they saw, over the course of roughly eight days of culture, was extraordinary.
The aggregates self-organized into structures that progressively looked more and more like real post-implantation mouse embryos.

By day 8.5 of culture — equivalent to day 8.5 of real mouse development, which is around when the body plan is being laid down — the structures had: a recognizable head fold, with the beginnings of a forebrain.
A neural tube extending along the body axis.
A beating heart.
Somites — the segmented blocks of mesoderm that will become vertebrae and ribs.
A yolk sac.
An allantois.
Blood islands beginning to form.
The early structures of essentially all the major organ systems of an early mammalian embryo.
Let me let that sink in.
From stem cells alone — no egg, no sperm, no fertilization — they grew a mouse synthetic embryo that, by day 8.5 in vitro, had a beating heart and a forming brain.

If you watch the videos that accompanied the papers, you see a translucent structure, a few millimeters across, with a clear head, a clear tail, segmented somites along the length of the body, and a tiny pulsating spot at the front where the early heart tube is contracting.

The structure is suspended in liquid, in a glass rotating bottle, hooked up to nothing biological — no maternal blood supply, no uterus, no placenta beyond the synthetic embryo's own self-generated extraembryonic membranes.
The whole thing is, as Hanna himself has put it, "a kind of self-contained mouse.
It's not a full embryo.
The synthetic embryos have anatomical drift — proportions are sometimes off, particular structures are missing or extra.
The efficiency is low — only a small fraction of starting aggregates make it to day 8.5 with normal-looking morphology.
And the structures cannot be implanted into a real mouse and continue developing, at least not in any consistent way.
The system has a ceiling, currently around day 8.5, beyond which the synthetic embryos fail.
Real mouse development goes to day 19 or 20 to birth; the synthetic ones get a little less than halfway there.
But they get to the heart.
They get to the beginnings of the brain.
They get the somites and the early circulation.
From stem cells.
This is the most striking demonstration the field has produced.
A few things to note about how this was done.
The naive pluripotent state is key.

Hanna's lab works extensively with naive pluripotent cells — cells in a state that more closely resembles the inner cell mass of a real blastocyst than the more conventional "primed" pluripotent cells most labs use.
Naive cells appear to be more capable of giving rise to both embryonic and extraembryonic lineages, which is part of why this approach works.
Other labs working with primed cells have had a harder time getting the same range of lineage output.
The mechanical incubator is critical.
Mouse embryos, in the uterus, develop in a rotating, well-perfused environment with constantly changing nutrient gradients.
The rotating bottle system mimics aspects of this — keeping the embryo from settling, ensuring gas exchange, allowing waste removal.
Without this kind of system, post-implantation development stops quickly.
Hanna's lab spent years developing this incubator, and it's a key piece of why their work goes as far as it does.
The signaling timing is crucial.
The cells need different cocktails at different stages.
Pluripotency maintenance early on, then differentiation cues, then morphogenesis-supporting signals.

Getting the timing right — when to switch from one medium to another, when to introduce particular factors, when to step back and let the structure self-organize — was the result of careful empirical work.
The structures are mouse, not human.
Crucially.
The Hanna 2022 and 2023 papers used mouse cells.

The ethical landscape is much simpler for mouse work — mice are research animals, mouse embryo research is regulated but allowed in most jurisdictions, mouse synthetic embryos do not engage human embryo ethics directly.
But everyone in the field knew, when these papers came out, that the obvious next step was to try the same approach with human cells.
And as we'll see in Segment 11, that step was taken in 2023.
The Hanna 2022 work crystallized, more than any prior paper, what was at stake in the field.
The mouse synthetic embryos are not just blastocysts.
They are post-implantation, gastrulated, organogenesis-stage structures.
They have hearts.
They have brains.
They were made from stem cells.
They were grown in a dish.
If the same recipe works with human cells — even partially — then the field has, in effect, created a way to study early human organogenesis without using human embryos.
That's an enormous scientific opportunity.
But it also means that the structures we're talking about are getting closer and closer to what most people, looking at them, would call embryos.
The line between "model" and "thing being modeled" is getting fuzzy, and Hanna's mouse work was the moment when the fuzziness became impossible to ignore.
We're going to spend the next two segments stepping back from the science to talk about the ethics, because the ethical questions, by 2022, had become as central to the field as the science was.
